A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
Introduction
This compilation of abstracts forms part of the system for collecting and
disseminating information on Court decisions and arbitral awards relating to
Conventions and Model Laws that emanate from the work of the United Nations
Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL). The purpose is to facilitate
the uniform interpretation of these legal texts by reference to international norms,
which are consistent with the international character of the texts, as opposed to strictly
domestic legal concepts and tradition. More complete information about the
features of the system and its use is provided in the User Guide
(A/CN.9/SER.C/GUIDE/1/REV.3). CLOUT documents are available on the
UNCITRAL website: (https://uncitral.un.org/en/case_law).
Each CLOUT issue includes a table of contents on the first page that lists the full
citation of each case contained in this set of abstracts, along with the individual
articles of each text which are interpreted or referred to by the Court or arbitral
tribunal. The internet address (URL) of the full text of a decision in its original
language is included in the heading to each case, along with the internet addresses,
where available, of translations in official United Nations language(s) (please note
that references to websites other than official United Nations websites do not
constitute an endorsement of that website by the United Nations or by UNCITRAL;
furthermore, websites change frequently; all Internet addresses contained in this
document are functional as of the date of submission of this document). Abstracts on
cases interpreting the UNCITRAL Model Arbitration Law include keyword
references which are consistent with those contained in the Thesaurus on the
UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration, prepared by the
UNCITRAL Secretariat in consultation with National Correspondents. Abstracts on
cases interpreting the UNCITRAL Model Law on Cross-Border Insolvency also
include keyword references. The abstracts are searchable on the database available on
the UNCITRAL website by reference to all key identifying features,
i.e. country, legislative text, CLOUT case number, CLOUT issue number, decision
date or a combination of any of these.
The abstracts are prepared by National Correspondents designated by their
Governments, or by individual contributors; exceptionally they might be prepared by
the UNCITRAL secretariat itself. It should be noted that neither the National
Correspondents nor anyone else directly or indirectly involved in the operation of the
system assumes any responsibility for any error or omission or other deficiency.

V.21-09502 3/15

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
Cases relating to the Model Law on Cross-Border Insolvency
(MLCBI)
Case 1933: MLCBI 2; 6; 15; 16(3); 17(2)(a); 21(1)
Australia: Federal Court of Australia
Palmer (Trustee), in the matter of Slater (Bankrupt) and Palmer (Trustee), in the
matter of Slater (Bankrupt) (No 2)
4 July 2016; 16 August 2016
Original in English
Published: [2016] FCA 780 and [2016] FCA 960
Abstract prepared by Stewart Maiden, National Correspondent
[Keywords: centre of main interests (COMI); foreign proceeding; foreign main
proceeding; foreign representative; presumption – COMI; presumption – habitual
residence; public policy; relief – upon request]
The applicant was appointed as the trustee of the insolvency estate of an Australian
citizen residing in the United Kingdom (the “debtor”) pursuant to the United Kingdom
Insolvency Act 1986. The debtor was adjudged bankrupt by an order of a United
Kingdom court in 2015 (the “UK proceeding”). Pursuant to s 6 of the Cross-Border
Insolvency Act 2008 of Australia (“CBI Act”) enacting the MLCBI, the applicant
commenced a proceeding in the Federal Court of Australia (the “Court”), seeking:
(1) recognition of the UK proceeding as a “foreign proceeding” within the meaning
of article 2(a) of the MLCBI; (2) recognition of the UK proceeding as a “foreign main
proceeding” within the meaning of article 2(b) of the MLCBI; (3) an order that the
administration and realization of the debtor’s assets located in Australia be entrusted
to two local trustees as local representatives of the applicant (the “Australian
representatives”); and (4) an order that all powers normally available to local trustees
in bankruptcy be made available to the Australian representatives.
In the proceeding of 4 July 2016 (FCA 780), the Court disagreed with the applicant
that the order adjudging the debtor bankrupt in the UK proceeding satisfied
requirements of article 15(2)(b) of the MLCBI. The Court required the applicant to
present a document referred to in either article 15(2)(a) or (b) of the MLCBI (“a
certified copy of the decision commencing the foreign proceeding and appointing the
foreign representative” or “a certificate from the foreign court affirming the existence
of the foreign proceeding and of the appointment of the foreign representative”,
respectively) or an affidavit which would verify that the UK proceeding is a “foreign
proceeding” within the meaning of the MLCBI. In the Court’s view, such an affidavit
would demonstrate that the applicant had considered and understood each element of
the definition of “foreign proceeding” for the purpose of satisfying herself that the
UK proceeding was indeed a “foreign proceeding”.
The Court also found that the evidence submitted by the applicant proving her
appointment in United Kingdom as the trustee of the debtor’s estate did not prove her
appointment as a foreign representative. Noting that the status of a “foreign
representative” would arise only in connection with a “foreign proceeding”, the Court
found that the status of the applicant as a “foreign representative” might become
sufficiently clear if the nature of the UK proceeding as a foreign proceeding was
established. If not established in accordance with article 15(2)(a) or (b) of the MLCBI,
the status of “foreign representative” might be established by an affidavit from the
applicant which verifies that she is a “foreign representative” within the meaning of
the MLCBI. Such an affidavit would demonstrate that the applicant had considered
and understood each element of the definition of “foreign representative” for the
purpose of satisfying herself that she is a “foreign representative”.
The Court thus granted leave to the applicant to file an amended application and
further evidence to demonstrate in particular that: (1) the UK proceeding was a
“foreign proceeding” within the meaning of article 2(a) of the MLCBI; (2) the
applicant was a “foreign representative” within the meaning of article 2(d) of the
4/15 V.21-09502

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
MLCBI; and (3) the applicant holds the opinion, on reasonable grounds, that an
additional relief under article 21(1)(g) of the MLCBI in the form of granting to the
Australian representatives all powers normally available to local trustees in
bankruptcy, would be necessary to protect the interests of the debtor’s creditors. The
Court adjourned the application to enable the applicant to file further evidence.
The applicant later filed an amended application, amending the statements made to
satisfy the requirements of article 15 of the Model Law and s 13 of the CBI Act. Based
on the amended application and further evidence submitted by the applicant, the
Court, on 16 August 2016, granted all four orders listed in the first paragraph above
(FCA 960).
Case 1934: MLCBI 21
Australia: Supreme Court of the Australian Capital Territory
Palmer v. Registrar-General of Land Titles of the Australian Capital Territory
22 December 2017
Original in English
Published: [2017] ACTSC 407
Abstract prepared by Stewart Maiden, National Correspondent
[Keywords: applicable law; assets of the debtor; foreign representative; private
international law; relief – upon request]
The case followed Palmer (Trustee), in the matter of Slater (Bankrupt) [2016]
FCA 780 and Palmer (Trustee), in the matter of Slater (Bankrupt) (No 2) [2016]
FCA 960 (see CLOUT case 1933). The plaintiff applied to the Supreme Court of the
Australian Capital Territory (the “Court”) for an order to correct the register of land
titles in the Australian Capital Territory by requiring and directing the Registrar-
General of Land Titles to remove the Australian representatives (who had been
appointed pursuant to article 21(1)(e) MLCBI) as the registered proprietors of the
debtor’s immovable property located in Australia and register herself as the registered
proprietor of that property.
The Court found that by the rules of private international law, immovable property
may be transferred only in accordance with the lex situs. With reference to the
applicable case law, the Court concluded that Australian law does not automatically
recognize the title of the plaintiff to the immovable property. That is notwithstanding
that the law of the foreign proceeding in which she was appointed as the trustee,
vested the trustee automatically with rights to all debtor’s assets, including those
located overseas, without any requirement for registration. The Court ruled that, in
the absence of a local court’s order, the plaintiff as the foreign trustee had no
immediate entitlement to have the property registered in her name and capacity to
take control of it for the purposes of the bankruptcy.
The Court disagreed with the plaintiff’s submission that because the foreign
proceeding in which she was appointed as the trustee was recognized in Australia, the
Cross-Border Insolvency Act 2008 (“CBI Act”) enacting the MLCBI in Australia
cloaked her with all general rights, powers and entitlements of a local trustee,
including entitlement to be registered as the proprietor of the debtor’s immovable
property located in Australia.
The Court held that the effect of the CBI Act and the MLCBI was to give to the foreign
representative the powers expressly contemplated by the Act and not to give
expressly, or to imply, any general power of administration of assets within Australia.
The Court compared types of relief available under the CBI Act and the MLCBI for
recognized foreign main and non-main proceedings under articles 19, 20 and 21 of
the MLCBI, noting in particular that, whereas the relief under article 20 was automatic
upon recognition of the foreign main proceeding, relief under articles 19 and 21 was
discretionary. The Court noted that types of relief available under article 21 operated
according to their terms and those terms did not suggest that they were merely
V.21-09502 5/15

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
“supplementary to some unstated general reservoir of power” that existed in a foreign
representative in the absence of the local court’s order.
The Court thus concluded that in the absence of the local court’s order, the plaintiff
did not have a general power or entitlement to be registered as the proprietor of the
immovable property in Australia solely by virtue of recognition of the foreign
proceeding. The Court recalled in that context that the 2016 order (see CLOUT
case 1933), as requested by the plaintiff in accordance with article 21(1)(e) of the
MLCBI, entrusted the administration and realization of the debtor’s assets in Australia
to the Australian representatives. It did not empower the plaintiff to become the
registered proprietor of the immovable property in question. The plaintiff’s
application was thus dismissed.
Case 1935: MLCBI 2; 16; 17; 21
Australia: Supreme Court of New South Wales
In the matter of Hydrodec Group Plc
24 June 2021
Original in English
Published: [2021] NSWSC 755
Abstract prepared by Stewart Maiden, National Correspondent
[Keywords: foreign proceeding; foreign main proceeding; foreign representative;
centre of main interests (COMI)]
The plaintiffs were a debtor company (the “Company”) incorporated in the United
Kingdom and registered as a foreign company in Australia and the joint monitors
appointed in the United Kingdom moratorium proceeding concerning the Company
(the “UK proceeding”).
The Company was also the subject of a winding up application brought in the Supreme
Court of New South Wales by an Australian creditor.
Pursuant to s 6 of the Australia Cross-Border Insolvency Act 2008 that enacted the
MLCBI, the plaintiffs commenced a proceeding in the Supreme Court of New South
Wales, seeking recognition of the UK proceeding under the MLCBI and an order
under article 21 MLCBI which (among other things) would stay the creditor’s winding
up proceeding. The plaintiffs sought only recognition as a foreign main proceeding,
not as a foreign non-main proceeding. In the alternative, they sought a stay of the
winding up proceeding under statutory provisions and common law principles which
required the New South Wales court to consider acting in aid of certain foreign
insolvency proceedings.
The Court found that the UK proceeding was a foreign proceeding as defined in
article 2(a) of the MLCBI. The UK proceeding was taking place in United Kingdom
pursuant to the Insolvency Act 1986, a law relating to insolvency. Premised on the
joint monitors’ opinion that the moratorium under the UK proceeding would likely
result in the rescue of the Company as a going concern, the UK proceeding was
collective in nature. Further, the Company was subject to control or supervision by a
United Kingdom Court, either directly or through the joint monitors who were officers
of the court.
The Court also determined that the joint monitors, not the Company, were the foreign
representatives as defined in article 2(d) of the MLCBI. Despite acknowledging that
the directors of the Company could still undertake some transactions without the joint
monitors’ consent, the Court noted that the joint monitors had a degree of control from
their ability to grant or withhold consent for transactions central to reorganization.
Such constraints over the directors’ dealings on behalf of the Company were
distinguishable from precedents where the custodian merely supervised, as opposed
to controlled, the debtor company.
The Court refused to recognize the UK proceeding as a foreign main proceeding
within the meaning of articles 2(b) and 17(2) of the MLCBI because it was found that
6/15 V.21-09502

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
the United Kingdom was not the centre of the Company’s main interests (COMI). As
a preliminary matter, the Court found that the presumption under article 16(3) of the
MLCBI (under which the COMI was presumed to be in the State in which the
company has its registered office) was rebutted. In the Court’s opinion, the
presumption did not apply in circumstances where (as in this case) the company had
registered offices in more than one country.
The Court identified the COMI by reference to objective criteria ascertainable by third
parties. The plaintiffs contended that the Company’s COMI was in the United
Kingdom, where the Company was incorporated, the registered office was located,
and the directors lived. Against the plaintiffs’ submissions, the Court determined that
the Company’s COMI was in the United States of America for the reasons that the
Company’s United States subsidiary was the only entity that traded and earned
revenue, that the United States subsidiary was wholly owned by the Company and
operated entirely within the United States, that the plaintiffs implicitly recognized the
critical importance of the United States subsidiary to the reorganization of the
Company, and that the administration of the affairs of the Company involved in
substance the administration of the operations of the United States subsidiary. The
Court observed that those matters were ascertainable by third parties based on
information published by the Company.
Consequently, the plaintiffs’ claim for recognition was refused, and their application
for article 21 relief was denied. In addition, the application for a non-MLCBI stay
was refused. The Court ordered that the Company be wound up in insolvency.
Case 1936: MLCBI 1; 2; 15; 17; 19; 20
Brazil: Tribunal de Justiça do Estado do Rio de Janeiro – Comarca da Capital
– 3ª Vara Empresarial
Case No. 0129945-03.2021.8.19.0001
Re: Prosafe SE
05 July 2021
Original in Portuguese
Abstract prepared by Henrique Forssell, Voluntary Contributor
[Keywords: centre of main interests (COMI); foreign main proceeding; international
obligations; presumption – foreign representative; relief – upon request]
The plaintiff sought recognition in Brazil of a foreign insolvency proceeding as a main
proceeding based on articles 167-H (enacting article 15 MLCBI) and 167-M (enacting
article 20 MLCBI) of the Insolvency Law of Brazil (“BIL”) and a request for
provisional relief staying the execution against the debtor’s assets on the basis of
article 167-L BIL (enacting article 19 MLCBI). The plaintiff had its headquarters in
Norway, but it controlled a group of enterprises having their seat in Singapore.
Restructuring proceedings were opened voluntarily by the company group in
Singapore pursuant to item 64 of the “Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act
2018”, for which it was granted “moratorium protection”.
The court found that the criteria for granting recognition of the foreign proceeding
was met. First, the judge mentioned the duties of judges and public authorities under
article 167-A BIL (enacting article 1 MLCBI) to cooperate in cross-border insolvency
cases. Second, it confirmed that the application for recognition contained all the
documents required under article 167-H BIL (enacting article 17 MLCBI), including
certified copies and official translations thereof. The judge accepted that
the proceedings opened in Singapore met the criteria for “foreign proceeding” of
article 167-B (1) BIL (enacting article 2(1) MLCBI), after verifying that the
proceedings were opened in accordance with national law of Singapore, placing the
assets of the company group under the administration of the Supreme Court of
Singapore.
As far as relief is concerned, the judge acknowledged the importance of a timely
decision when it comes to moratorium protection, in order to protect the company
V.21-09502 7/15

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
against creditors. The court found that the requirements of article 167-L
[corresponding to article 19 MLCBI] and article 300 of the Code of Civil Procedure
of Brazil were fulfilled and accepted the plaintiff’s request.
With regards to the recognition of the Singapore proceeding as a foreign
main proceeding, the judge rebutted the presumption of centre of main interests
(COMI) being the place of registered office under article 167-I BIL [corresponding to
article 16(3) MLCBI] and recognized the Singaporean proceeding as a foreign main
proceeding. The judge considered that the company, although headquartered in
Norway, controlled at least five enterprises in Singapore, made most of its business
there and was recognized by the creditors as having its place of business in that
jurisdiction.
Having recognized the Singapore proceeding as a foreign main proceeding, by virtue
of articles 167-M and 167-N (v) [corresponding to articles 20 and 21(g) MLCBI], the
decision granted the automatic stay of execution against debtor’s assets, the
interruption of the prescription period for any executory actions against the plaintiff
and the ineffectiveness of transfer, encumbrance or any form of disposal of
non-current assets of the debtor, carried out without prior judicial authorization.
Case 1937: MLCBI 2; 21
New Zealand: High Court of New Zealand
ANZ National Bank Ltd v. Sheahan and Lock, in the matter of Ex Ced Foods
(formerly Cedenco Foods) (in liq) and Cedenko Ohakune (in liq)
15 November 2012
Original in English
Published: [2012] NZHC 3037
Abstract prepared by Patricia Keeper, National Correspondent
[Keywords: foreign proceeding; relief – upon request]
The respondents were the Australian and New Zealand liquidators of a group of
companies owning companies in Australia and New Zealand. Those companies’ major
source of finance was the first applicant (the “applicant”) and its branch in Australia.
The Australian liquidators, in their roles as liquidators of the Australian companies
made an application to the High Court of New Zealand (the “Court”) seeking an order
that a bank officer employed by applicant be required to attend for examination and
produce documents on matters relating to the Australian companies. The application
was made under article 21(1)(d) of the First Schedule to New Zealand’s Insolvency
(Cross-Border Act) 2006 (“ICBA”), which enacted the MLCBI.
The Australian liquidators argued that the Court had a discretion to order an
examination under article 21(1)(d) of the MLCBI because the liquidation of each
Australian company had been recognized by the High Court in 2011 as a foreign main
proceeding in New Zealand. While there were no New Zealand decisions in which an
order for examination has been made under article 21(1)(d), after full argument, the
Court looked to a United Kingdom decision (Chancery Division of the High Court,
Picard v. FIM Advisers LLP (see CLOUT 1942)) dealing with the equivalent article
in the English Cross-Border Insolvency Regulations. The United Kingdom Court
interpreted the equivalent article 21 as having a jurisdictional and a discretionary
component; in exercising that discretion, courts must consider all relevant
circumstances and ensure that the interests of the person against whom the order is
sought are adequately protected.
The Court then applied the Picard holding to the present case. There was jurisdiction
to bring an application under article 21 because a foreign proceeding had been
recognized. Regarding the discretionary considerations, the Court balanced
competing considerations. The Court recognized there were genuine issues to be
explored concerning the cross-guarantees given to the applicant entities on behalf of
the group of companies to guarantee each other’s obligations, for the benefit of the
Australian companies. The Court believed denying the application would impair the
8/15 V.21-09502

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
Australian companies’ ability to bring a civil claim against the applicant due to
relevant limitation periods. While the applicant acknowledged the benefits, it cited
the definition of “foreign proceeding” in article 2 of the MLCBI and argued that the
MLCBI was intended to provide assistance to foreign representatives to realize assets
of an insolvent debtor for the benefit of its creditors as opposed to a foreign
representative of a solvent company. The Court rejected the applicant’s reasoning on
the grounds that article 2 still characterized the regime as a “collective ... proceeding
... pursuant to a law relating to insolvency” where an orderly regime is imposed upon
all interested parties.
However, those genuine issues were balanced against the fact that any examination
would reveal information that might not otherwise be available to the Australian
companies were those companies to issue proceedings against the applicant in that
jurisdiction, or the applicant in New Zealand. While the considerations were finely
balanced, the Court was concerned with the absence of any evidence of whether an
Australian Court under Australian domestic law could order an examination by the
Australian liquidators. If the Australian Court would not have granted an examination,
then granting an order for examination in New Zealand would provide an unnecessary
and undesirable forensic advantage to the Australian liquidators. In light of the
circumstances, the Court adjourned the application pending rulings from the
Australian Court.
Case 1938: MLCBI 20; 21
Singapore: Court of Appeal
Civil Appeal No. 10 of 2021
United Securities Sdn Bhd (in receivership and liquidation) and another v. United
Overseas Bank Ltd
10 August 2021
Original in English
Published: [2021] SGCA 78
Abstract prepared by Sim Kwan Kiat, National Correspondent
[Keywords: recognition; collective proceeding]
The respondent had granted a loan to the first appellant, a company incorporated in
Malaysia. The loan was secured by a fixed charge over the first appellant’s shares in
another company. The first appellant was later wound up by the Malaysian court (the
“Malaysian Winding-up Proceeding”).
The first appellant applied to the Malaysian court for a declaration that the respondent
was not entitled to certain funds as they were not subject to the charge over shares
(the “Malaysian Proceeding”). The respondent applied to the Singapore court for a
declaration concerning its rights on the charge over shares and the same funds (the
“Singapore Proceeding”).
The first appellant applied to the Singapore court for recognition of the Malaysian
Winding-up Proceeding as a foreign main proceeding or foreign non-main proceeding
under MLCBI, and consequently, a stay of the Singapore Proceeding pursuant to
article 20 or 21 of MLCBI as enacted in Singapore via s 252 of the Insolvency,
Restructuring and Dissolution Act (Act 40 of 2018).
The Court of Appeal upheld the decision of the High Court in declining to grant a stay
of the Singapore Proceeding on the basis that the ambit of such stay under the
Singaporean enactment of the MLCBI is the same as what would have been available
under Singapore law had the debtor been wound up in Singapore, and would be
subject to the same exceptions and conditions under Singapore law. In addition, it was
stated that, under the Singaporean enactment of the MLCBI, such stay does not affect
the rights of creditors to take steps to enforce their security over the debtor's property.
Under Singapore law, the court will generally permit secured creditors to enforce their
security over the debtor’s property, notwithstanding any stay of proceedings that
V.21-09502 9/15

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
arises upon the winding up of the debtor. In this case, the Singapore Proceeding was
directed at allowing the respondent to establish its rights as a secured creditor. As
such, notwithstanding the recognition of the Malaysian Winding-up Proceeding, the
Court of Appeal allowed the respondent to proceed with the Singapore Proceeding.
The Court of Appeal also declined to grant a discretionary stay of the Singapore
Proceeding pursuant to article 21 MLCBI as it was not necessary to protect the
property of the first appellant or the interests of the creditors. The respondent was,
prima facie, a secured creditor, and its security stood apart from the pool of assets
available for distribution amongst unsecured creditors.
In passing, the Court of Appeal considered whether the Malaysian Proceeding could
be recognised as a foreign proceeding under MLCBI. To constitute a foreign
proceeding under MLCBI, a proceeding must involve creditors collectively; must
have its basis in a law relating to insolvency; the court must exercise control or
supervision of the property and affairs of the debtor in the proceeding; and the purpose
of the proceeding must be the debtor's re-organisation or liquidation. As the Malaysian
Proceeding bore none of these attributes, it would not be regarded as a foreign
proceeding under MLCBI.
Case 1939: MLCBI 17(1); 21(1)
United Kingdom: England and Wales High Court of Justice, Business and Property
Courts, Insolvency and Companies List (Chd)
Case No: CR-2016-004824 & CR-2020-0003146
In the matters of: (1) Industria de Alimentos Nilza SA; (2) Buglin Participacoes
LTDA; and (3) Endipa Comércio e Administração LTDA
21 December 2020
Original in English
Published: [2020] EWHC 3560 (Ch)
Abstract prepared by Irit Mevorach, National Correspondent
[Keywords: foreign proceeding; foreign main proceeding; foreign representative;
recognition; relief – upon request]
The applicant applied to the High Court for: (1) recognition of two applications
extending the foreign proceeding to two other Brazilian companies (collectively the
“Companies”) (the “Extension Applications”); (2) recognition of the applicant as the
foreign representative; and (3) relief compelling the respondent to disclose documents
related to the Companies, under the common law or pursuant to the Cross-Border
Insolvency Regulations 2006 (the “CBIR”) enacting the MLCBI in Great Britain.
In relation to recognition pursuant to the MLCBI, noting that the Extension
Applications do not have a parallel under English law, the court was nevertheless
satisfied that the likely pooling of the Companies’ assets did not preclude the
Extension Proceedings from being “collective proceedings” for the purposes of the
CBIR. The court found that other requirements for recognition of the Extension
Applications under article 17 of the MLCBI were also satisfied and that no public
policy exception applied. In addition, the court found that no contrary evidence
existed to rebut the presumption that the centre of the Companies’ main interests was
in the place of the Companies’ registered office, i.e., in Brazil. Consequently, the
Extension Applications were recognised as foreign main proceedings to which the
respondent was recognised as the foreign representative.
In relation to relief pursuant to the MLCBI, the court noted that article 21(1)(g) of the
MLCBI enabled the respondent to apply for any relief available to an English
insolvency officeholder. Accordingly, the court, satisfied that the respondent
may indeed hold information concerning the Companies, granted relief pursuant to
article 21(1)(g) of the MLCBI requiring the respondent to disclose to the applicant
any documents and information it holds regarding the Companies, including
documents which it holds on behalf of a third party that relate to the Companies’
affairs.
10/15 V.21-09502

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
Case 1940: MLCBI 17(2); 21
United Kingdom: England and Wales High Court of Justice, Business and Property
Courts, Insolvency and Companies List (ChD)
Case No. BR-2021-000044
In the matter of Georgy Ivanovich Bedzhamov
13 August 2021
Original in English
Published: [2021] EWHC 2281 (Ch)
Abstract prepared by Irit Mevorach, National Correspondent
[Keywords: foreign proceeding; centre of main interests (COMI); recognition; relief
– upon request; scope – MLCBI]
Recognition was sought in England of: (1) a Russian bankruptcy order made against
the respondent; (2) the applicant as the respondent’s bankruptcy trustee; and (3) relief
entrusting immovable property in England in the applicant (collectively the
“Recognition Application”).
Notwithstanding the fact that the Recognition Application was requested under the
common law, the High Court construed the wording of the relief sought as to have
been borrowed from article 21 of Schedule 1 of the Cross-Border Insolvency
Regulations 2006 (the “CBIR”), enacting the MLCBI in Great Britain. Thus, the Court
considered the possibility for recognition under the CBIR before considering
recognition under common law.
The Court emphasized that recognition under article 17(2) MLCBI only applied to
insolvency proceedings taking place in the country in which the debtor has the centre
of its main interests (COMI) or where the debtor has an “establishment” as defined in
the MLCBI. However, given that, on the facts, the debtor had neither COMI nor any
form of “establishment” in Russia, the Court held that recognition under the MLCBI
was unavailable. Moreover, the court made clear that relief under article 21
MLCBI was contingent upon recognition of a foreign proceeding in accordance with
article 17 MLCBI. Thus, with such recognition unavailable, the court also declined to
grant relief under the MLCBI.
After having found that none of the bars to common law recognition applied, the court
recognised both the Russian bankruptcy order against the respondent and the applicant
as the respondent’s bankruptcy trustee under the common law but dismissed the
request for relief considering that there was no English precedent for granting it.
Case 1941: MLCBI 12; 17; 21
United Kingdom: Court of Appeal, Civil Division
Case No. A2/2019/0703
In the matter of Candey v. Crumpler
23 January 2020
Original in English
Published: [2020] EWCA Civ 26
Abstract prepared by Irit Mevorach, National Correspondent
[Keywords: recognition; foreign proceeding; foreign representative; relief]
This case deals with the effect of a recognition order of a foreign liquidation
proceeding under Schedule 1 of the Cross-Border Insolvency Regulations 2006 (the
“CBIR”) enacting the MLCBI in Great Britain.
The debtor is a company established in the British Virgin Islands (“BVI”) that was
involved in prolonged litigation and was represented by solicitors from the appellant.
Later, the debtor entered liquidation in the BVI courts, and the liquidators (“the
respondents”) subsequently applied to the High Court of England and Wales for the
recognition of the BVI liquidation pursuant to the CBIR. A recognition order was
issued recognizing the BVI liquidation as the foreign main proceeding. During the
V.21-09502 11/15

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
liquidation, a dispute arose between the respondents and the appellant about the
appellant’s claim to unpaid fees owed to it by the debtor.
Following one of the hearings, the High Court ordered the respondents to pay the
appellant some of its legal costs, but not the success fees due to the general prohibition
of their recovery in England and Wales save in some specific circumstances, which
include when proceedings in England and Wales are brought by a person acting in the
capacity of a liquidator of a company which is being wound up in England and Wales
under parts IV and V of Insolvency Act 1986. The High Court declared that the BVI
liquidation, although recognized as the foreign main proceeding in England, is not
covered by that exemption and the recognition of the BVI liquidation as the foreign
main proceeding does not provide the foreign representative with the capacity to act
as a liquidator of a company in England and Wales. The High Court thus ruled that
the success fees were not recoverable. Among other issues, this decision was
appealed.
The Court of Appeal considered the grounds for the exemption issue and the scope of
the recognition of foreign proceedings. The court interpreted article 21 in conjunction
with articles 12, 17, 20 and 23 of the MLCBI as enacted in the CBIR. The court
explained that upon recognition, the foreign representative may participate in local
insolvency proceedings regarding the debtor. Recognition also does not affect the
right to request to initiate proceedings under the domestic insolvency law provisions
(Insolvency Act of 1986 in the United Kingdom). Various relief may be sought.
However, nothing in the structure or wording of the MLCBI as enacted in the CBIR
supports the argument that a recognized foreign representative is to be treated as a
British insolvency officeholder or that it acts in the capacity of a British insolvency
officeholder. Based on the analysis of the domestic law containing the exemption, the
court concluded that the law did not allow a foreign representative to be treated as “a
person acting in the capacity of a liquidator of a company being wound up in England
and Wales”. Therefore, the appeal was dismissed.
Case 1942: MLCBI 21(1); 22
United Kingdom: England and Wales High Court of Justice, Chancery Division,
Companies Court
Case No. 11283 of 2009
Picard (Foreign Representative of Bernard L. Madoff Investment Securities LLC) v.
FIM Advisers LLP
27 May 2010
Original in English
Published: [2010] EWHC 1299 (Ch)
[Keywords: assets of the debtor; foreign representative; relief – upon request]
The applicant made an application for an order pursuant to Schedule 1 of the Cross-
Border Insolvency Regulations 2006 (“CBIR”) enacting the MLCBI in Great Britain,
article 21 requiring the respondent to produce documents concerning the assets,
affairs, rights, obligations, and liabilities of the debtor.
The High Court considered that article 21(1)(d) of CBIR [corresponding to
article 21(1)(d) MLCBI] has both a jurisdictional and a discretionary component. The
court must be satisfied that the information sought concerned the debtor’s assets,
affairs, rights, obligations or liabilities and, if it was so satisfied, then it had discretion
to order the delivery of that information. In exercising that discretion, it must have
regard to all relevant circumstances and ensure that the interests of the person against
whom the order was sought were adequately protected. Reference in that regard was
made to article 22 of CBIR [corresponding to article 22 MLCBI] that provides that in
granting or denying relief the court must be satisfied that the interests of interested
persons are adequately protected. The High Court noted that interested persons
included the person against whom an order for delivery of information is sought.
12/15 V.21-09502

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
The High Court was satisfied that the information sought could indeed concern the
assets, affairs, rights, obligations, or liabilities of the debtor. In exercising discretion
as regards granting the relief sought, the High Court considered in some detail the
date range for the search, the locations to be searched and several disputed categories
of documents. The court found that the need for the trustee to discharge its duties,
including investigating the conduct, property, liabilities and financial conditions of
the debtor, outweighed oppression on the respondent. Pursuant to article 21(1)(d)
CBIR, the High Court thus ordered the respondent to conduct an internal search of all
relevant information within the date range and the locations specified in the order.
The High Court was not prepared to make an order for disclosure of the documents
from back-up systems, noting however that it would be sympathetic to order such a
disclosure if the ongoing disclosure exercise proved fruitful; hence the process of
restoration of the documents on the back-up systems should be completed so that any
further order for disclosure can be addressed without delay.
Case 1943: MLCBI 17(2); 21(1)
United States of America: Bankruptcy Court, Southern District of New York
Case No. 19-12908 (MEW)
In re Reward Science and Technology Industry Group Co., Ltd.
8 October 2019
Original in English
Abstract prepared by John Pottow, National Correspondent, and Allan Gropper,
Voluntary Contributor
[Keywords: centre of main interests (COMI); foreign proceeding; recognition]
The debtor, a company registered under the laws of China, had commenced a
proceeding under the Enterprise Bankruptcy Law of China, before the People’s Court
of Chaoyang District, Beijing Municipality. The court-appointed insolvency
representative filed a petition before the United States courts under Chapter 15 of
title 11 of the US Bankruptcy Code (enacting the MLCBI) to obtain recognition of
the proceeding in China in the United States as a foreign main proceeding.
The Court, having verified its jurisdiction and being satisfied that the centre of the
debtor’s main interest as defined in Section 1502(4) of the Bankruptcy Code (enacting
article 2(b) MLCBI) was in China, concluded that the Beijing proceeding pending in
China was a foreign main proceeding pursuant to Section 1517(b)(1) of the
Bankruptcy Code (enacting article 17(2)(a) MLCBI).
Accordingly, the Court ordered that the “foreign representative and the debtor will be
entitled to the full protections and rights enumerated under Sections 1521(a)(1)
and (4)-(6) of the Bankruptcy Code” (enacting article 21(1)(a) and (d)-(f) MLCBI).
In addition, the Order issued by the Court required that the creditors shall, before
commencing any enforcement action or self-help remedy against the debtor or its
assets outside the United States or China, provide the foreign representative with prior
written notice setting forth with specificity the action contemplated as well as the
precise location of where the action is anticipated to occur.
The Court ordered that the debtor shall not transfer, sell, or assign any assets identified
in the notice provided by the creditors. The Court also reminded to the debtor of its
obligation to inform the creditors of any enforcement action or self-help remedy taken
by any other creditor against the debtor or any of its property outside the United States
and China. Eventually, the Court established the foreign representative as the
representative of the debtor with full authority to administer the debtor’s assets and
affairs in the United States (para. 8, see also article 21(1)(e) MLCBI).

Case 1944: MLCBI 2; 6; 7; 15; 19; 20; 21; 22; 25
United States of America: Bankruptcy Court, Northern District of Illinois, Eastern
Division
Case No. 20bk18167
In re Condor Flugdienst GmbH
26 March 2021
Original in English
Published: 627 B.R. 366
Abstract prepared by John Pottow, National Correspondent, and Allan Gropper,
Voluntary Contributor
[Keywords: comity; creditors; foreign main proceeding; interpretation; recognition;
recognition – application for; relief – automatic; relief – upon request]
Foreign representatives of a company based in Germany applied for the recognition
and implementation of a confirmation order of a liquidation plan issued by a
German court and to grant related relief based on 11 U.S.C.§§ 105(a), 1521(a)
(enacting article 21 MLCBI), 1525(a) (enacting article 25 MLCBI) and 1527
(enacting article 27 MLCBI).
The representatives initially filed a petition for recognition of a liquidation proceeding
initiated by German Courts as a foreign main proceeding as defined in § 1502(4) of
the US Bankruptcy Code (enacting article 2(b) MLCBI) and for grant of provisional
relief (§ 1519 enacting article 19 MLCBI). At a Provisional Hearing in October 2020,
a general stay of proceedings against the company’s assets was granted. At a
Recognition Hearing in November 2020 the Court recognized the German
proceedings as a foreign main proceeding, which triggered the automatic protections
of §1520 U.S. Bankruptcy Code (enacting article 20 MLCBI) and stayed all
actions against the company in the United States pursuant to § 1521(a)(1) (enacting
article 21(1)(a) MLCBI).
While the recognition request was still pending before the United States Court, the
German Court entered an order confirming the Insolvency Plan of the company. The
Foreign representatives then petitioned the recognition of the foreign confirmation
order and the implementation of the Plan in the United States. American creditors
raised objections against recognizing the insolvency plan on the basis of several
provisions of chapter 15 of the US Bankruptcy Code (enacting MLCBI).
In deciding the case, the court discussed that, even if there is disagreement over the
basis on which a court can recognize and enforce foreign plans, the authority
undoubtedly exists. It found that the authority to grant non-discretionary relief can be
derived from the following sources: in chapter 15 of the US Bankruptcy Code, it is to
be found in §1507(a) (enacting in substantially amended form, article 7 MLCBI); in
the principle of “additional assistance” in §1521 (enacting article 21 MLCBI), by
which a court can grant any appropriate relief provided that “sufficient protection” is
given to creditors, as required by section 1522; and in section 105(a) of the US
Bankruptcy Code, by which courts can “issue any order, process or judgement that is
necessary or appropriate to carry out the provisions of the Bankruptcy Code”.
After looking at the legislative history of the MLCBI and its Guide to Enactment and
Interpretation, in particular the interdependence between article 21 of MLCBI and
article 7 as enacted by the United States, the court concluded that it had power to
enforce the foreign plan under section 1521, as long as sufficient protection was
provided to creditors under section 1522. It further concluded that there was no need
to access the additional powers granted to a court under section 1507, as the Court
had done in the Vitro case.
Finally, the Court analysed the objections raised by local creditors against the foreign
insolvency plan. It was concluded, among other things, that the interests of the
creditors were respected and there were no grounds for rejecting the liquidation plan,
since (1) the foreign proceedings provided relief akin to that available in United States
14/15 V.21-09502

A/CN.9/SER.C/ABSTRACTS/211
bankruptcy law and the foreign proceedings need not be identical to United States
proceedings to be recognized in the United States; (2) there was no difference in the
treatment provided to foreign as opposed to local creditors and it was not unduly
burdensome to require United States creditors to submit claims in Germany; and
(3) creditors were given notice two times, the first time in accordance to Germany’s
due process requirements, the second time in accordance to notice requirements of
article 1514 Bankruptcy Code (enacting article 14 MLCBI).
In the end, the court was satisfied with the treatment creditors were afforded in the
German liquidation proceedings and the motion for the recognition and
implementation of a confirmation order of a German liquidation plan was granted. In
addition to that, to effectuate the German plan, the court permanently enjoined
prepetition litigation commenced by some creditors.
